title: Effective increase of a superconducting critical temperature in a high-entropy electron mixture
abstract: We show theoretically that a superconducting critical temperature can be effectively increased in a high-entropy mixture of electrons belonging to conduction and valence bands. In order to employ the entropy of mixing into the superconducting phase dynamics, we suggest to use a metallic trap that removes quasiparticle excitations from the superconductor. This makes the concentration of Cooper pairs a dynamic variable of the entropy of mixing, and thus affects the Ginzburg-Landau functional of the superconductor effectively reducing the first expansion coefficient or, in other words, increasing the critical temperature.
summary: This work theoretically demonstrates that the entropy associated with mixing conduction and valence electrons can boost the superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$). This is achieved by modeling the system with a metallic trap that dynamically controls the Cooper pair concentration, thereby modifying the Ginzburg-Landau free energy functional.
main_problem: The paper theoretically investigates how the superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$) can be effectively increased in a high-entropy electron mixture.
main_result: The entropy of mixing contributes to the Ginzburg-Landau functional by modifying the first expansion coefficient ($\alpha$), which effectively increases the predicted critical temperature.
method: The authors use the Ginzburg-Landau functional formalism, incorporating the entropy of mixing ($\Delta S$) and modeling the removal of quasiparticle excitations using a metallic trap.
model_or_system: The system is a solid solution of two compounds forming a high-entropy electron mixture, modeled by coupling the superconducting free energy to the entropy of mixing and the dynamics of quasiparticle trapping.
key_observables: Superconducting order parameter ($\Delta$), Ginzburg-Landau coefficients ($\alpha,  eta$), Higgs mode spectrum gap energy, and London penetration depth ($\lambda_L$).
important_parameters: Entropy of mixing ($\Delta S$), quasiparticle trapping/relaxation rates ($\Gamma_t, \Gamma_e, \Gamma_r$), and the coherence length ($\xi_0$).
main_assumptions: The metallic trap efficiently removes quasiparticle excitations ($n_n \approx 0$), and the system is treated using approximations valid away from the superconducting transition temperature.
figures_summary: Figure 1 schematically illustrates the setup: a bulk superconductor coupled to a metallic film designed to trap quasiparticle excitations.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by first deriving the entropy of mixing, incorporating it into the Ginzburg-Landau functional via a metallic trap, and then analyzing the resulting renormalized coefficients and spectral properties (like the Higgs mode gap).
why_interesting: The coupling of thermodynamic entropy (a statistical mechanics concept) directly into the superconducting order parameter via quasiparticle dynamics provides a novel mechanism for enhancing superconducting properties.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic picture of a setup consisting of a bulk supercon- ducting material that contains two types of atoms (blue and purple) which are randomly ordered in a crystal lattice, and a metallic film (grey) attached to the superconductor via a tunnelling barrier. The metallic film traps quasiparticles from the superconductor. The elec- trons of the Nv purple atoms contribute to the valence band, while the electrons of the Nc blue atoms are donated into the conduction band.

FIG. 2. Schematic picture of the densities of states of a 2D metallic trap (νN) and a 2D superconductor (νS) with respect to renormalized energy E/|∆|. The corresponding rates in their junction: Γt is the trapping rate of a quasiparticle moving into the metallic film, Γe is the escape rate into the superconducting film, and Γr is the relaxation rate of an obtained quasiparticle, now a metallic one, into a lower energy state. In order to have an effective relaxation, there should be many empty states below |∆| in the metal, i.e., the temperature should be enough low. The shaded areas show the densities of states of a metallic trap νN = m∗/π, with m∗= 1 here, and of a super- conducting film...

FIG. 3. Evolution of the Ginzburg-Landau functional of the super- conducting sample. The evolution of the free energy (black circles) is noted via black arrows. The green line shows the initial Ginzburg- Landau functional of the sample at a certain temperature T0, de- scribed by α and β and the full ∆S. Then we turn on a metallic trap, that absorbs quasiparticles and consequently normal electrons nn. Thus, the energy T0∆Sn = kBT0nn ln (n/nc) is added to the sample (red line). The decrease of energy of electrons in a metal- lic trap should compensate this increase in energy.
